prohibitedCollection: credentials, cookies, tokens, or recovery codes
real trip notes or fishing history
raw quotes, recordings, or participant-level transcripts
participantBoundary.volunteeredSensitiveDataResponse: Interrupt collection, do not copy or summarize the value, ask the participant to return to the fictional scenario, and exclude the affected response from any tally.

interviews.title: What the Opportunity Score means
interviews.scenario: Show a fictional screen where Site A has an Opportunity Score of 82 and Site B has 61 for the same species and time window.
prompts: In your own words, what does the score of 82 mean here?
Would you read 82 as an 82 percent chance of catching a fish, and why?
What does this comparison not tell you?
What would you verify somewhere else before deciding whether to go?
expectedConcepts: The score is a relative comparison within the displayed options.
The score is not a catch probability or promise of an outcome.
The person still needs current safety, access, regulation, and condition checks.

interviews.title: Reading source freshness
interviews.scenario: Show a fictional forecast card with one source marked updated two hours ago and another marked stale.
prompts: Which part of this screen tells you how current the information is?
What would you do after seeing that one source is stale?
Does a recently updated source guarantee that the displayed conditions are correct, and why?
What wording or visual treatment would make freshness easier to understand?
expectedConcepts: Each source can have a different observation or update time.
A stale warning should reduce reliance and prompt an outside check.
Recent data can still be incomplete, delayed, or different from local conditions.

interviews.title: Understanding limitations and outside checks
interviews.scenario: Show a fictional planning card that includes a relative rank, uncertainty copy, and links to official sources.
prompts: Does this screen tell you that a place is safe, legal, open, or accessible?
Which facts would you verify with official or local sources?
What uncertainty remains after reading the explanation?
How would you describe the planner's role to another person?
expectedConcepts: The planner does not certify safety, legality, access, or open status.
Official regulations, forecasts, notices, and local conditions require separate checks.
The planner helps compare options rather than making the final decision.

interviews.title: Trip-report privacy expectations
interviews.scenario: Show a fictional pending trip report and explain that only a human-approved aggregate may become public; no real report should be entered during the interview.
prompts: What would you expect other visitors to see from this pending report?
Which information should never become public?
Would you expect the pending report itself to be public before human review, and why?
What privacy explanation would you want to see before submitting a future report?
expectedConcepts: A pending report remains private and cannot publish automatically.
Raw notes, identity, precise coordinates, and photos do not belong in a public summary.
Any public output requires explicit human approval and privacy-preserving aggregation.

interviews.title: Making a decision from the whole screen
interviews.scenario: Show two fictional windows with different relative ranks, source ages, uncertainty messages, and official-source links.
prompts: Which information would you look at first, and why?
What would make you pause before relying on the higher-ranked option?
Which decisions can this screen support, and which decisions must stay with you?
How would you explain the score, freshness, limitations, and privacy boundaries in one sentence?
expectedConcepts: Rank, freshness, uncertainty, and outside verification must be considered together.
A higher rank does not override stale data or safety, access, and legal checks.
The product supports planning while the person retains responsibility for the trip decision.
